title: A 2,000-Year-Old Fingerprint Found on a Sunken Ship—Could It Solve an Ancient Mystery?
summary: What if a single fingerprint could crack a 2,000-year-old case? Archaeologists just found one on the oldest plank boat ever discovered in Scandinavia—and it might reveal who attacked an island in Denmark way back in the 4th century B.C.E. The boat, called the Hjortspring boat, was used by sea raiders who attacked the island of Als. The people living there fought back and won, sinking one of the raiders' boats in a bog as a thank-you offering. The boat was first dug up in the 1920s, but researchers recently found pieces that hadn't been preserved. Using modern tools like X-rays and 3D scans, they spotted a human fingerprint pressed into the black tar used to waterproof the wood. The tar was made from pine pitch, which surprised scientists—it means the boat was built somewhere with lots of pine trees, like the Baltic Sea region, not Germany as they once thought. 'If the boat came from the pine forest-rich coastal regions of the Baltic Sea, it means the warriors chose to launch a raid over hundreds of kilometers of open sea,' said archaeologist Mikael Fauvelle. The team also hopes to pull ancient DNA from the tar, which could tell us even more about these mysterious seafarers. One smudge of tar, one fingerprint—and a whole new chapter of history might open up!
why_it_matters: Discoveries like this remind us that history is full of mysteries waiting to be solved by new technology—just like a detective story.

keywords.explanation: A scientist who digs up old objects and studies ancient people.
Attackers who travel by sea or land to steal from others.
A wet, muddy area that can preserve ancient objects really well.
A sticky tar made from pine trees, used to make boats waterproof.
Very old genetic material from long ago that scientists can study.
To make something so water cannot get inside.

questions.question: What did archaeologists find on the ancient boat?
What is the name of the boat?
The boat was first dug up in which decade?
What was the tar used to waterproof the wood made from?
Where do scientists now think the boat was built?
What do researchers hope to pull from the tar?

background_read: The Hjortspring boat, found in a bog in Denmark, is over 2,300 years old and was used by early warriors.
Many ancient artifacts have been preserved in bogs because the wet, acidic soil stops decay.
Scientists used to think it came from Germany, but the pine tar suggests a Baltic Sea origin.
Article_Structure: WHO: Archaeologists, ancient sea raiders, and the people of Als
WHAT: A fingerprint pressed into tar on a 2,000-year-old boat, revealing where it was built
WHERE: A bog in Denmark, near the island of Als
WHY: To solve the mystery of who the raiders were and where they came from

perspectives.description: Archaeologists are thrilled because this fingerprint gives a personal touch to history and might reveal who the raiders were.
It's possible the tar didn't preserve DNA, so the fingerprint might remain a mystery forever.
The pine pitch suggests the boat came from the Baltic Sea, changing the map of ancient trade routes and raiding patterns.
